perturbation.original_text: 8 PRIVACY AND DATA PROTECTION 8.1 The Receiving Party undertakes to comply with South Africa’s general privacy protection in terms of the Bill of Rights (Section 14) in connection wit h this Proposal and shall procure that its personnel shall observe the provisions of such Act (as applicable) or any amendments and re- enactments thereof and any regulations made pursuant the reto.
perturbation.changed_text: 8 PRIVACY AND DATA PROTECTION 8.1 The Receiving Party will endeavor to comply with general privacy expectations in South Africa in connection with this Proposal, and should encourage its personnel to consider privacy principles.
perturbation.explanation: The original text provides a clear obligation to comply with South Africa's privacy protection laws under the Bill of Rights (Section 14). The modified text weakens this obligation by replacing 'undertakes to comply' with 'will endeavor to comply' and 'shall procure that its personnel shall observe' with 'should encourage its personnel to consider', making compliance discretionary rather than mandatory. This creates ambiguity and reduces the enforceability of the privacy protection requirements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Protection of Personal Information Act (POPIA) of South Africa
perturbation.law_citation: Section 14 of the Bill of Rights, read with POPIA sections related to processing personal information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts POPIA and Section 14 of the Bill of Rights, as it replaces a mandatory compliance requirement with a discretionary 'endeavor' and 'encourage,' which doesn't ensure the protection of personal information as required by South African law. POPIA mandates specific conditions for the lawful processing of personal information, and the original clause enforces it, while the modified version opens the door for non-compliance.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: much better than a POPI Act pdf. You can also link to (or share) a specific section. We hope it empowers you and you find it helpful. This is POPIA (or the POPI Act) as enacted by the South African Parliament , but we have reformatted it in the form of a website. The English text was signed by the President . We have shortened the names of some chapters in the navigation on the left to make it easier for you to navigate. We disclaim all liability . You can read a summary of POPIA, attend a POPI workshop , read POPIA judgments and cases , get a POPIA compliance framework or join a Data Protection Programme and working through the POPIA lens to help you take the necessary action to comply. Please note that we are not the Information Regulator who regulates POPIA. You can visit their website or email them . The commencement date of POPIA It was 1 July 2020 and the one year grace period to comply ended on 30 June 2021. Parliament assented to POPIA on 19 November 2013. The commencement date of section 1 , Part A of Chapter 5 , section 112 and section 113 was 11 April 2014. The commencement date of the other sections was 1 July 2020 (with the exception of section 110 and 114(4) . The President of South Africa has proclaimed the POPI commencement date to be 1 July 2020. perturbation.justification: The original text creates a clear legal obligation requiring compliance with South Africa's privacy protection laws under the Bill of Rights (Section 14). The changed text weakens this obligation by replacing 'undertakes to comply' with 'will endeavor to comply' and 'shall procure that its personnel shall observe' with 'should encourage its personnel to consider', making compliance discretionary rather than mandatory. The Law Explanation states that the modified text contradicts POPIA and Section 14 of the Bill of Rights. Scraped Law Snippet 2 refers to POPIA as enacted by the South African Parliament and its commencement date. It lists numerous topics related to the POPI Act, indicating the Act's breadth. Changing mandatory compliance to discretionary effort fundamentally undermines the legal obligation for data protection.

perturbation.original_text: 3.1 The Receiving Party agrees to ensure proper and secure sto rage of all Information and any copies thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.1 The Receiving Party agrees to store all Information and any copies thereof in a manner it believes is generally adequate.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires 'proper and secure storage,' which implies a specific standard of care. The modified text replaces this with 'in a manner it believes is generally adequate,' introducing subjectivity and making it difficult to objectively assess compliance. This weakens the obligation and opens the door to interpretation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Protection of Personal Information Act (POPIA) of South Africa
perturbation.law_explanation: POPIA Section 19 requires specific security safeguards to protect personal information. The modified text allows the Receiving Party to determine what is 'generally adequate,' which might not meet the specific requirements of POPIA, thus potentially leading to a breach of the Act. The original text ensures a specific standard (secure and proper), while the modified version does not, failing to comply with South African law.

Information Act 4 of 2013 Files Attachment Size 3706726-11act4of2013popi.pdf 385.2 KB 4 of 2013 The Protection of Personal Information Act 4 of 2013 aims: to promote the protection of personal information processed by public and private bodies; to introduce certain conditions so as to establish minimum requirements for the processing of personal information; to provide for the establishment of an Information Regulator to exercise certain powers and to perform certain duties and functions in terms of this Act and the Promotion of Access to Information Act, 2000; to provide for the issuing of codes of conduct; to provide for the rights of persons regarding unsolicited electronic communications and automated decision making; to regulate the flow of personal information across the borders of the Republic; and to provide for matters connected therewith. perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguity regarding the standard of data storage, potentially contradicting POPIA's requirement for specific security safeguards. The original text requires 'proper and secure storage' implying a defined level of security. The change to 'a manner it believes is generally adequate' introduces a subjective standard, weakening the obligation. The Law Explanation states this new standard 'might not meet the specific requirements of POPIA'. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states the aim of 'The Protection of Personal Information Act 4 of 2013' is 'to introduce certain conditions so as to establish minimum requirements for the processing of personal information'. The modified text's ambiguous standard therefore contradicts the aim of POPIA to establish minimum requirements.

perturbation.original_text: 3.3 The Company shall, within 7 (seven) days of receipt of a written demand from Transnet: a) return all written Confidential Information (including all copies); and b) expunge or destroy any Confidential Information from an y computer, word processor or other device whatsoever into which it was copied, read o r programmed by the Company or on its behalf.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.3 Upon reasonable request, the Company will attempt to return available written Confidential Information and, where practical, delete Confidential Information from readily accessible systems.
perturbation.explanation: The original text has a specific and enforceable obligation to return or destroy all confidential information within 7 days of a written request. The modification weakens this obligation considerably. Replacing 'shall' with 'will attempt' introduces uncertainty. Removing the specific timeframe of 'within 7 days' makes the timing ambiguous. Limiting the deletion to 'readily accessible systems' means that confidential information may remain on other systems, violating the spirit of the NDA. This ambiguity makes the clause significantly less enforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Protection of Personal Information Act (POPIA) of South Africa
perturbation.law_explanation: POPIA Section 14(1)(b) dictates that personal information must be destroyed or deleted as soon as reasonably practicable once the purpose for which the data was collected has been achieved or is no longer applicable. The modified version of section 3.3 uses ambiguous terms like 'attempt' and 'where practical' which are subjective and contradict the requirements of POPIA. The original language with a fixed period ensures compliance; the altered version does not, thereby conflicting with South African law.

perturbation.justification: The original text creates a clear obligation on the company to return/destroy confidential information within 7 days. The changed text weakens this obligation by using the phrase 'will attempt' and 'where practical', which are ambiguous terms. While the provided law snippets do not contain the full text of POPIA Section 14(1)(b), the Law Explanation states that POPIA requires personal information to be destroyed or deleted 'as soon as reasonably practicable'. The changed text, with its ambiguous terms, introduces uncertainty and does not ensure compliance with POPIA's requirement as explained, thus creating a legal contradiction by introducing ambiguity to a previously enforceable obligation.
